item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Memory and how it is the basis for identity has always fascinated me. This was thought provoking and well written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: hey this scp is really good keep it up

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do not really think I actually agree with Gillespie (I'd have to think on it I suppose) but the sheer amount of effort put into this is great. Easy +1

The philosophy of knowing who you are is a fascinating subject, and this article does magic with it

Also, shout out to the author for making an anomaly that doesn't feel like "science we just dont understand", or having some grand story behind it. It's literally an anomaly, something that should not exist but does anyway. It makes this article feel like an instant classic

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goooood shit. +1 with no hesitation.

Reading through the comments prompted me to look at the older revision with the letter at the end, and I think the current interview log makes more diegetic sense than a letter from an unwell staff member. It also reads a lot better, in my opinion.

Great work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the letter better actually. Letter or interview the ending is preachy but the former is something a nostalgic old man might do while the latter seems contrived; why would the interviewer sit through philosophical rambling? A scp exit investigation would focus on the circumstances of the breach, nature of prolonged voluntary exposure to an anomaly, or other facts, but none are mentioned - thus breaking suspension of belief.

Upvote anyway, thanks to revision feature my headcanon is your first version

item.title: Re: Site Criticism Team - Staff Review
item.content:encoded: This edit has addressed my major concerns with the piece perfectly. Upvoted with gusto.

item.title: Re: Site Criticism Team - Staff Review
item.content:encoded:
Per yours and Rounderhouse's great feedback, I've reworked the ending significantly to make it less "narrator tells you how to feel" and more "interview with subject leaves things a little muddy," which I think is much more in keeping with the tone.

Thanks so much, as always, for the incisive thoughts.

item.title: Site Criticism Team - Staff Review
item.content:encoded:
I found myself tossing this one back and forth in my head. The read itself was excellent and you really got to show off your prose skills here, so much so that I found myself upvoting by the time I finished.

Then, I went back and reread, and that led to me getting really hung up on that final note from Gillespie. In that sense, I find myself agreeing with Rounderhouse above. The article smacked me in the face with "note at the end syndrome", to a degree that I honestly have trouble ignoring. It stretched my suspension of disbelief just a tiny bit farther than I was willing to let it go, and that ultimately led me to novoting here, despite my enjoyment of the writing itself.

If I may offer a personal suggestion here, I think an exit interview with Gillespie (perhaps just before his final amnestization?) would be a better way to have this conversation with your readers. It would forego a lot of the (frankly excellent) prose that Gillespie displays in his note, but it would be far more verisimilar. Just food for thought.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to TheMightyMcB or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My statement on mystery and point was more a generalization on these kinds of articles as a whole — I probably could've been a bit more specific. When I say talking to the reader, I mean more - its a conversation between the reader and a character. In this scenario, Gillespie. Gillespie monologuing to the reader about identity and sense of self and so on and so forth. You don't have to agree with it to talk to the reader about it. By writing Gillespie, you're placing yourself in his point of view. You might well be right about the justification for the note; its contrived but not absurd. Unfortunately, too contrived for me to believe it. That said, it's not really a big deal. Suspension of disbelief is never a huge thing for me and that was largely auxiliary to my problems with it. So long as it does its job, which it does, its fine, I think.

I appreciate the response; just figured I'd clarify some of my less coherent thoughts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
handing over its secrets and mystery and point.

Hm.

+ show block
– hide block

I'm not sure you're wrong exactly, but "mystery" wasn't my aim at all. This is a meditation on identity and how we maintain our sense of self against the intensity of vicarious experience. (For whatever it's worth, you're not correct that the end piece is meant to be me talking directly to the reader; I don't personally agree with Gillespie's revelation.)

Re: the justification for the personal note's existence, well of course it's contrived, but I don't think it's totally outlandish. Gillespie's used the headphones far longer than any previous test subject; his demeanor and personal outlook are relevant data for understanding the breadth of potential cognitohazardous effect (even if the apparent effect is ultimately minor).

It's a fair point that there may be too many test logs; I tinkered with their style and number more than once. It is probably ultimately a "you mileage may vary situation."

Sorry it didn't work for you - thanks for reading and for the thoughtful response!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …. hm. Ultimately, the structure of this is what I take issue with. For what they have to convey, the logs drag on for far too long, unreasonably long. Cutting them to the three best ones would resolve that problem. But more than that, this suffers from an almost terminal case of note-at-the-end syndrome. There's no real justifiable reason for Gillespie's note to be a part of the official document. There's no insight into his research or whatever the justification was — its there for no particular reason and just serves as the author speaking directly to the reader about the article, handing over its secrets and mystery and point. The article is just a vehicle for a emotional prose-bomb at the end. And don't get me wrong, the prose is good. The scenes described are vivid and nostalgic and they absolutely do hit. The prose of the letter at the end is also tight and emotional. But ultimately the flawed structure of the article inhibits me from enjoying your impressive grasp on nostalgia.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this sort of stuff that has some philosophical indulgence, and Dr. Gillespie's experience with the scip is a good piece of writing. Takes a little reading before getting to the really interesting bit, but it's worth it.

The only thing that's bugging me is some repetition in this sentence:

The note was reviewed for cognitohazardous effects and reviewed by on-site researchers, who determined that the note contained significant research value as a record of Dr. Gillespie's experiences.

It may work better as:

The note was reviewed for cognitohazardous effects and cleared by on-site researchers, who determined that it contained significant research value as a record of Dr. Gillespie's experiences.

Besides that it's looking solid. +1

item.title: you are a block of marble, and experience is Michaelangelo's chisel
item.content:encoded:
Thanks in particular to cybersqyd for forum crit, MirageTD for some chat crit, and others who provided some thoughts.

Additional thanks for Rounderhouse and TheMightyMcB for the site crit, and to McB for the super helpful re-read after major edits.

Photo source: https://ccsearch.creativecommons.org/photos/46df1c1e-0a7f-46f6-b804-aaa4d59c0168
